name: Avenc de sa Soca
starting_point: Parking at Nuestra Señora de la Paz and restaurant Mirador de Na Burguesa (Génova). Follow the path past the radio station antennas towards Coll des Pastors. After the first high-voltage tower (on your left), climb a small pass and when the descent ends, leave the path — the pit is 800 meters away, off-trail.
equipment: Ropes: 1x20 m, 1x25 m, 1x70 m, several slings and carabiners for 4-5 fractionations.
description: A 103-meter deep pit in the Serra de Na Burguesa near Calvià, the second deepest known in the area, formed by tectonic fractures rather than water dissolution. The descent involves steep slippery ramps with flowstone floors and narrow passages requiring careful rope work. Formations include stalactites, curtains, stalagmites, columns, and soda straws, with the best decoration below 50 meters. Designated as a site of speleological, recreational, and biological interest.
full_description:
The morphology of the pit we are going to discuss maintains similarity with the characteristics of others in the area, among which is the Avenc de l'Infern (GRACIA 1997). The numerous pits found in the Serra de Na Burguesa owe their formation to fractures in the terrain, which makes them cavities entirely different from those existing in areas such as Escorca or Pollença, where water is the cause of their formation through the process of dissolution.

One can install on a pine tree in the surroundings to begin the vertical descent to the Sala d'Entrada (Entrance Chamber), the only place — with the exception of the deepest part of the pit — where we can maintain an upright position.

The floor of this chamber is covered with stones, earth, blocks, and tree remains, and it is also a place where life appears to be very present. Notable are multiple specimens of the spider Meta minardi, as well as chilopods (centipedes), and at the time of our visit, a specimen unknown to me of a sporocarp (fungus), for further identification. The formations in this chamber or vestibule are mainly covered with earth, and the formations that can be found in the form of wall flowstone are extremely scarce. A few meters from the main entrance, a second opening appears, impassable due to the obstruction of a rock, but it is not inconceivable that in the future there could be two accesses to the pit. The continuation of the cavity is at the southeastern end of the chamber in the form of a narrow crack that allows us to pass through crawling without major difficulty. Perhaps we can say that the cavity is divided into two parts: the entrance chamber and the rest. The first ramps are not too steep, being about 47° (CRESPÍ 2001); the floor is covered with earth and blocks at first, later becoming a slippery place also due to how wet the earth is, so the use of progression equipment can save us from a scare. Indeed, as we descend, we can observe practically the ends of the cavity where some cracks appear that lead nowhere. Formations are scarce as observed so far until reaching approximately -50 m, where on our right a corner seems to break the pattern and shows the first concretions worth mentioning.

On the main descent, the slope increases to 50° (CRESPÍ 2001). The entire floor is covered with pavement flowstone bathed in a thin layer of water that gradually soaks our clothing. Along the way, we will encounter a couple of ledges where we can, if necessary, wait for our companions. The concretion is greater at the level of the second ledge and at lower elevations until reaching the -103 m level, where blocks of stone accumulate on the floor along with the impracticable continuation of the cavity, as a block of stone prevents it. Attempting to continue without clearing the obstruction is impossible, but what is certain is that once stones are thrown, everything suggests they descend quite a few meters.

Clastic processes are clearly visible in the lower part of the cavern, where large slabs have detached from the ceiling and threaten to fall on us, were it not for the fact that they are propped up by other blocks. As for the lithochemical processes, we can find a great variety, including stalactites — some of them eccentric — curtains, stalagmites, columns, soda straws, and wall and pavement flowstone. Solifluction processes also occur in this cavity.

Great care must be taken with loose stones as they can fall on us, so when one person is descending, no one should be below unless they are in good shelter.

In summary, a cavity that exceeds one hundred meters in depth — not many reach that depth in the area; in fact, it is the second of such dimensions known to date — which despite not being rich in formations has enough to give us a good time contemplating and photographing them. At no point does the visit lose interest, as its low ceiling keeps us continuously alert, sometimes having to overcome very narrow sections, and we will certainly not emerge to the outside too clean. If the visit is well utilized, the Avenc de sa Soca can occupy us for a full day, but if that is not the case, less than thirty meters away is the claustrophobic Cova des Corbs (463465 4379473 ED50), and a little further away the Avenc des Cans (463478 4379538 ED50).

The Avenc de sa Soca is considered a site of speleological, recreational, and biological interest.
